1: Three crimes of Moab — the crime is desecration of the dead: be-sarfo atsmot melek-Edom la-sid (because he burned the bones of Edom's king to lime). Sid (lime, whitewash) — the bones were burned to produce caustic lime, either for use in construction or as a final act of desecration. The victim is the king of Edom — the same Edom who appears as perpetrator in Amos 1:6, 9, 11. The criminal irony: Moab committed this atrocity against Edom, yet YHWH holds Moab accountable. International justice operates above ethnic alliances.
2: Fire on Moab — ve-met Moav basha'on (Moab will fall/die in turmoil) — sha'on (tumult, uproar) complements the battle imagery. Beteri'ah bequol shofar (with shouts and the sound of the ram's horn) — the cultic-military horn (shofar) is both the alarm of battle and the instrument of judgment.
3: Cut off the ruler — shofet (ruler, judge) is Moab's title here vs. yashav for the Philistine cities; the term may reflect the type of governance. The princes (sarim) fall together with the leader — total governmental collapse.
4: Three crimes of Judah — the oracle turns from foreign nations to the covenant people. The crime is specifically framed in covenant terms: me'asam et-Torat YHWH (they rejected the instruction/Torah of the LORD) and chuqqav lo shamar (they did not keep his statutes). Toehu (their false gods) — to'im (wanderings/falsehoods) — the idols are named by what they cause: straying, wandering. The formulaic crime-accusation shifts from specific atrocities (foreign nations) to covenant-failure (Judah), escalating the moral framework.
5: Fire on Judah — Ve-shilachti esh biYehudah: Judah receives the same fire-formula as the pagan nations. Armunot Yerushalam (fortresses of Jerusalem) — the capital's defensive structures face the same fate as Damascus, Gaza, and Tyre. The symmetry is deliberate and shocking: covenant status does not exempt from covenant-curse.
6: Three crimes of Israel — the climactic oracle, twice as long as the others. The crime list begins with economic: makhram ba-keseph tsaddiq ve'evyon ba'avur na'alayim (they sell the innocent for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals). Tsaddiq (innocent, righteous person) — someone who should prevail in a legal dispute but is sold out; evyon (needy/poor one) — the technical term for the destitute class. Ba'avur na'alayim (for sandals) — trivially small debt used to justify enslaving a person (cf. Nehemiah 5:1-5).
7: Trample the heads of the poor — ha-sha'afim 'al-afar erets be-rosh dallim: sha'af (trample/pant after) combined with the image of pushing the head of the poor into the earth-dust (afar). Ve-derekh anavim yattu (push the afflicted off the road) — natah derekh (bend/divert the path) as a technical metaphor for denying legal process. The second accusation: ve-ish u-aviv yelkhu el-hanna'arah (father and son go to the same young woman) — sexual exploitation either of cult prostitutes or of female slaves, both violating the prohibition of Leviticus 18:15. Lechalel et-shem qodshi (to profane my holy name) — the climax: social sin profanes the divine name.
8: Pledged garments — begadim chavolim: clothes taken as debt-collateral (chaval = pledge) that were forbidden to be kept overnight (Exodus 22:25-26; Deuteronomy 24:12-13). Spreading them out etsel kol-mizbeach (beside every altar) is doubly condemned: the stolen pledge is used in cultic celebration. Yeshet anushim (wine from court fines) — wine purchased with extorted legal settlements, consumed in the very house of God. The sacred space is profaned by the proceeds of economic injustice.
9: Yet I destroyed the Amorite — YHWH shifts to first-person recitation of saving history, contrasting past acts of grace with present ingratitude. Emori (Amorite) = the pre-Israelite Canaanite population collectively. The hyperbolic description — ka'asher gavohah aruzim gobho (whose height was like the cedars) and chazaq hu ke-allonim (strong as the oaks) — is mythologizing rhetoric emphasizing the supernatural difficulty YHWH overcame. Va'ashamed peryov mima'al ve-shorasho mittachat (I cut off his fruit above and his roots below) — total uprooting: neither fruit (offspring) nor root (lineage) survived.
10: I brought you up from Egypt — the exodus formula introduces the covenant basis for heightened accountability. Va'ohel etchem ba-midbar arba'im shanah (I led you through the wilderness forty years) — the wilderness period as sustained divine provision. Lareshet et-erets ha-Emori (to possess the land of the Amorite) — the conquest as the fulfillment of the exodus; YHWH's acts are a unified sequence (exodus → wilderness → conquest) that Israel owes entirely to divine initiative.
11: I raised up prophets and Nazirites — YHWH's provision was not only material (land, food) but prophetic. Nazirites (nezurim) — those under a vow of consecration (Num 6): no wine, no cutting of hair, no contact with the dead. Raising up both prophets and Nazirites represents both word and life-witness. The rhetorical question — ha-lo-khen bene Yisra'el? (is this not so, O people of Israel?) — demands acknowledgment of the gracious provision.
12: But you gave the Nazirites wine — the two provisions are deliberately violated: ve-tashqu et-haNezurim yayin (gave the Nazirites wine) and ve-'al-haNevi'im tsivitem le'mor lo tinnave'u (ordered the prophets: do not prophesy). Both acts are institutional silencing of divine communication: corrupting the life-witness of the Nazirite and gagging the verbal witness of the prophet. The people actively suppressed the very means of grace YHWH provided.
13: I will crush you in your tracks — the punishment oracle uses agricultural imagery. Hinneh anochi me'iq tachetechem — meiq is hapax; most derive from 'uq (press, oppress) or 'iq (make to groan). The image: YHWH himself as the crushing weight. Ka'asher ta'iq ha'agalah ha-mele'ah lah (as a cart loaded with sheaves presses down) — the threshing cart's impossible load pressing into the ground; Israel has loaded YHWH's patience past its limit.
14: Flight will fail the swift — three paired failure-of-escape vignettes: swift (kal) finds no flight, strong (chazaq) finds no strength, warrior (gibbor) saves not his life. The irony is that military capacity — speed, strength, skill — is exactly what fails. Each verse introduces another category of military professional rendered helpless.
15: The bowman will not hold his ground — ve-tofa'-qeshet lo ya'amod: continuing the cascade of military failure. Tofa' qeshet (handler of the bow) — ranged weaponry that should allow escape also fails. Qal beregalav lo yimalet (the swift-footed will not escape) — speed again inverted; parash susim lo yemallet nafsho (horseman will not save his life) — even mounted cavalry cannot outrun divine judgment.
16: The most courageous warrior will flee naked — ve-amits libbo ba-giborim (the most courageous of heart among the warriors) — the superlative: the bravest of the brave. Arom yanus bayom hahu (will flee naked on that day) — arom (naked): stripped of armor, of dignity, of everything. Ba-yom hahu (on that day) — the Day of the LORD formula appears here for the first time in Amos, linking military defeat to the eschatological judgment theme developed in Amos 5:18-20.
